---
title: "Reshaping"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 6
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch5 — Joins & groupby"
learning_objectives:
  - "Tell wide data apart from long (tidy) data and say when each is useful"
  - "Reshape a table between the two layouts with melt and pivot"
  - "Summarise repeated values into a wide grid with pivot_table and an aggregate"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 18
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
  - ../../lib/data.py
  - data/penguins.csv
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: wide-vs-long
    q: "What is the difference between wide and long (tidy) data?"
    a: "Wide data spreads one variable across many columns (one row per subject, a column per measurement). Long (tidy) data stacks those measurements into rows: one column names the variable, another holds the value, so each row is a single observation."
  - id: what-melt-does
    q: "What does df.melt() do?"
    a: "It reshapes wide to long: it gathers the measurement columns into two columns — 'variable' (the old column names) and 'value' (the cell contents) — keeping any id_vars columns as identifiers. The result has one row per value."
  - id: what-pivot-does
    q: "What is the difference between pivot and pivot_table?"
    a: "pivot reshapes long back to wide and needs one value per index/column cell. pivot_table does the same but first applies an aggregate (mean, sum, count, …), so it can summarise many values that land in the same cell."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Picture a class register on paper. Across the top run the subjects — *Maths*,
*Science*, *English* — and down the side run the students. Each cell holds one
score. It is compact and easy to read at a glance: that is **wide** data, one row
per student with a column for every subject.

Now picture the same marks typed into a logbook, one line at a time: *Asha, Maths,
80*. *Asha, Science, 91*. *Ravi, Maths, 72*. Every line is a single score with its
labels beside it. That is **long** data — taller, more repetitive to read, but far
easier for a computer to group, filter, and chart.

Neither layout is "right." They are the same numbers in two shapes, and real work
constantly moves between them: a colleague hands you a wide spreadsheet, your
plotting library wants it long, your report wants it wide again. This chapter is
about that move. Two pandas tools cover almost all of it — **`melt`** turns wide
into long, and **`pivot`** turns long back into wide — with **`pivot_table`**
adding a summary step when several values share a cell.

> You'll need from before: `groupby` and split-apply-combine (M3 Ch5). A
> `pivot_table` is groupby's close cousin — a grouped summary arranged into a grid.

---

## Wide vs long, side by side

Let's make the class register concrete. Here it is as a **wide** table: one row
per student, one column per subject.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Wide data: one row per student, a column for each subject"
import pandas as pd

wide = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Asha", "Ravi"],
    "math": [80, 72],
    "science": [91, 65],
})
wide
```

This is comfortable for a human reader. But notice a hidden awkwardness: the
*subject* is not stored as data — it is hidden in the column **names**. To ask
"what was the average score across all subjects?" you have to know which columns
are subjects and which (`student`) is not. That is exactly the question that long
data makes easy.

In **long** (also called **tidy**) form, every row is one observation — one
student, one subject, one score:

| student | subject | score |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Asha    | math    | 80    |
| Asha    | science | 91    |
| Ravi    | math    | 72    |
| Ravi    | science | 65    |

The table is taller and repeats `Asha` and `Ravi`, but now *subject* is a real
column you can group by, filter on, and feed to a chart. The rule of thumb for
tidy data is: **each variable is a column, each observation is a row.** Most pandas
tools — `groupby`, plotting, many models — are happiest with data in this shape.

## `melt`: wide → long

`df.melt()` gathers columns into rows. You tell it which columns are
**identifiers** to keep as-is (`id_vars`), and it stacks every *other* column into
two new columns: one holding the old column names, one holding the values.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "melt stacks the subject columns into variable/value rows"
import pandas as pd

wide = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Asha", "Ravi"],
    "math": [80, 72],
    "science": [91, 65],
})
long = wide.melt(id_vars="student", var_name="subject", value_name="score")
long
```

Read what happened: `student` stayed an identifier, and the two measurement
columns (`math`, `science`) were unpivoted into a `subject` column (the old names)
and a `score` column (the old values). Two rows became four — one per score. The
`var_name` and `value_name` arguments rename the two new columns; without them
pandas calls them `variable` and `value`.

Now a question like "average score per subject" is a plain `groupby`, because
`subject` is real data:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Long data makes a per-subject summary a one-liner"
import pandas as pd

wide = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Asha", "Ravi"],
    "math": [80, 72],
    "science": [91, 65],
})
long = wide.melt(id_vars="student", var_name="subject", value_name="score")
long.groupby("subject")["score"].mean()
```

That returns `math 76.0` and `science 78.0`. Try writing the same summary against
the wide table and you'll feel the difference — you'd have to name each subject
column by hand. Reshaping first, then summarising, is a pattern you'll reuse
constantly.

## `pivot`: long → wide

`pivot` is the reverse of `melt`. It spreads one column's values out into new
columns. You give it three things: the column to use as the row index, the column
whose values become the new column **headers**, and the column that fills the
**cells**.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "pivot spreads the long table back into a wide grid"
import pandas as pd

long = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Asha", "Asha", "Ravi", "Ravi"],
    "subject": ["math", "science", "math", "science"],
    "score": [80, 91, 72, 65],
})
back = long.pivot(index="student", columns="subject", values="score")
back
```

We are back to a student-by-subject grid. `melt` and `pivot` are a matched pair:
one tall-ifies, the other wide-ns. The catch with `pivot` is that it expects
**exactly one value** for each index/column combination. If two rows wanted to land
in the same cell — say Asha had two maths scores — `pivot` raises an error, because
it would not know which one to keep. That is where `pivot_table` comes in.

## `pivot_table`: long → wide, with a summary

`pivot_table` works like `pivot` but adds an **aggregate** step. When several
values fall into the same cell, it combines them with a function you choose
(`aggfunc`) — `"mean"`, `"sum"`, `"count"`, and so on. Think of it as a `groupby`
whose result is laid out as a grid.

Here are six sales rows, with repeats: the North store has two `tea` lines.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "pivot_table aggregates the repeats — total units per store and product"
import pandas as pd

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "store": ["North", "North", "North", "South", "South", "South"],
    "product": ["tea", "tea", "coffee", "tea", "coffee", "coffee"],
    "units": [10, 6, 4, 8, 5, 7],
})
sales.pivot_table(index="store", columns="product", values="units", aggfunc="sum")
```

The two North `tea` rows (10 and 6) were summed into a single cell of **16**;
South `coffee` (5 and 7) became **12**. A plain `pivot` could not have done this —
it would have choked on the duplicate `(North, tea)` pair. Swap `aggfunc="mean"`
and the same grid reports averages instead of totals. The default `aggfunc` is
`"mean"`, so always state the one you want to be sure.

If you stripped the column layout away, `pivot_table` is doing exactly what
`sales.groupby(["store", "product"])["units"].sum()` does — the same
split-apply-combine from Chapter 5, arranged as a rectangle that is easy to
read and to drop into a report.

### A quick pass on real data

The same move works on the penguins table. Each penguin has a `species` and an
`island`; `pivot_table` can count how many penguins sit in each
species-by-island cell in one call:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "pivot_table on penguins: a count of birds per species and island"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.pivot_table(index="species", columns="island", values="body_mass_g", aggfunc="count")
```

The grid shows where each species was seen — Gentoo only on Biscoe, Chinstrap only
on Dream, Adelie across all three — with `NaN` marking empty
combinations. One line turned 344 rows into a compact cross-tab.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. **Wide** data spreads a variable across columns (one row per subject); **long**
   (tidy) data stacks it into rows, one observation per row. Same numbers, two
   shapes.
2. **`melt`** reshapes wide → long, gathering measurement columns into a
   `variable`/`value` pair while keeping `id_vars` as identifiers.
3. **`pivot`** reshapes long → wide, but needs exactly one value per cell;
   **`pivot_table`** adds an `aggfunc` so it can summarise repeats into each cell.
4. Tidy (long) data is what `groupby`, plotting, and most models expect, so
   reshaping first then summarising is an everyday pattern.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.
The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

When you `melt` a wide DataFrame (with no `id_vars`), every cell becomes its own
row. Complete `long_row_count` so it returns how many rows the melted table has.
Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_long_row_count
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_long_row_count
import pandas as pd

def long_row_count(data):
    """Build a DataFrame from data, melt it, and return the long row count."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt().shape[0])

run_tests([
    ("2x2 wide", long_row_count({"a": [1, 2], "b": [3, 4]}), 4),
    ("one column", long_row_count({"x": [1, 2, 3]}), 3),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Build the table with `pd.DataFrame(data)`, call `.melt()` on it, and read
`.shape[0]` — that is the row count. Wrap it in `int(...)` so the return type is a
plain integer. With no `id_vars`, melt stacks *every* cell, so the long row count
the long row count is the rows times the columns.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def long_row_count(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt().shape[0])
```

`pd.DataFrame(data).melt()` unpivots every column into `variable`/`value` rows, one
row per original cell; `.shape[0]` counts those rows and `int(...)` returns a plain
integer. A 2×2 wide table has four cells, so it melts to four rows — rows times
columns.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

After a `melt`, the original column names live in the `variable` column. Write
`n_variables` to report how many **distinct** original columns there were — the
number of unique values in `variable`. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_n_variables
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_n_variables
import pandas as pd

def n_variables(data):
    """Melt the data and count how many distinct original columns it had."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt()["variable"].nunique())

run_tests([
    ("three cols", n_variables({"a": [1], "b": [2], "c": [3]}), 3),
    ("one col", n_variables({"x": [1, 2]}), 1),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Melt the frame, select the `"variable"` column (it holds the old column names),
and call `.nunique()` to count its distinct values. Wrap the result in `int(...)`.
Each original column name appears once per row it filled, but `.nunique()` counts
each name only once.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def n_variables(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt()["variable"].nunique())
```

After `melt`, the `variable` column repeats each old column name once per value.
`.nunique()` counts the distinct names, so three original columns give `3`
regardless of how many rows they held.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Take a small wide table — say a
few days across two outlets — `melt` it to long, then `pivot` it back to wide.
Confirm you recover the **original shape** by comparing `.shape` before and after.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Builds a small wide DataFrame, then melts it to long with `melt` (keeping a
  sensible `id_vars` column as the identifier).
- Pivots the long table back to wide with `pivot`, using the identifier as the
  index and the melted `variable` column as the new headers.
- Compares the original and round-tripped shapes (e.g. prints both `.shape`
  tuples) and states whether they match.

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Keep one column (like `day`) as `id_vars` when you melt, so you have something to
pivot back on. After pivoting, the index column moves into the row labels; use
`.reset_index()` to bring it back as a column before comparing shapes, and remember
`pivot` leaves a name on the columns axis you can clear.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

wide = pd.DataFrame({
    "day": ["Mon", "Tue"],
    "cafe": [12, 9],
    "kiosk": [7, 5],
})
print("original shape:", wide.shape)

long = wide.melt(id_vars="day", var_name="outlet", value_name="cups")
back = long.pivot(index="day", columns="outlet", values="cups").reset_index()
back.columns.name = None  # drop the leftover 'outlet' axis name

print("round-tripped shape:", back.shape)
print("shapes match:", wide.shape == back.shape)
```

The melt turns the two outlet columns into long `outlet`/`cups` rows; the pivot
spreads them back into a day-by-outlet grid. After `reset_index`, the recovered
table has the same `(2, 3)` shape as the original — a clean round trip that shows
`melt` and `pivot` are inverses.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch06.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now move a table between wide and long on demand: `melt` to tidy it for
grouping and charting, `pivot` to lay it back out for a report, and `pivot_table`
to summarise repeats into a clean grid. Reshaping is the quiet skill that makes the
rest of your pandas work flow — most "I can't get this into the right form" moments
end with one of these three calls. That wraps the core data-wrangling toolkit;
next we turn from shaping data to telling its story with charts.
